section.title: The Mystery of the Gospel
section.sectionRationale: Having declared that Jew and Gentile are made one new humanity and one temple in Christ, Paul pauses to unfold the mystery behind it. He was entrusted with a stewardship of grace to make known a plan hidden for ages and now revealed: that through the gospel the Gentiles are fellow heirs, fellow members, and fellow partakers in Christ. This mystery is not private knowledge for insiders but God's public purpose, displayed even to the powers in the heavenly realms through the church.
section.thesis: The once-hidden, now-revealed mystery is that through the gospel the Gentiles share fully in Christ with Israel; to make this known God gave Paul, the least of the saints, a stewardship of grace, so that through the church the manifold wisdom of God might be displayed to the rulers and authorities, according to His eternal purpose accomplished in Christ, in whom we approach God with boldness.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Paul the prisoner and steward of the mystery revealed to him; the mystery itself, that the Gentiles are fellow heirs through the gospel; and the church as the theatre where God's manifold wisdom is displayed to the heavenly powers, giving believers bold access to God.

units.label: Paul, prisoner and steward of the mystery

verses.text: For this reason I, Paul, the prisoner of Christ Jesus for the sake of you Gentiles...
Surely you have heard about the {{oikonomia|stewardship}} of God's grace that was given to me for you,
that is, the mystery made known to me by revelation, as I have already written briefly.
In reading this, then, you will be able to understand my insight into the {{mysterion|mystery}} of Christ,

units.label: The mystery revealed: Gentiles fellow heirs

verses.text: which was not made known to men in other generations as it has now been revealed by the Spirit to God's holy apostles and prophets.
This mystery is that through the gospel the Gentiles are {{synkleronomos|fellow heirs}}, fellow members of the body, and fellow partakers of the promise in Christ Jesus.
I became a servant of this gospel by the gift of God's grace, given me through the working of His power.

units.label: The church displaying God's manifold wisdom

verses.text: Though I am less than the least of all the saints, this grace was given me: to preach to the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ,
and to illuminate for everyone the stewardship of this mystery, which for ages past was kept hidden in God, who created all things.
His purpose was that now, through the church, the {{polupoikilos|manifold}} wisdom of God should be made known to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms,
according to the eternal {{prothesis|purpose}} that He accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord.
In Him and through faith in Him we may enter God's presence with {{parresia|boldness}} and confidence.
So I ask you not to be discouraged because of my sufferings for you, which are your glory.

oikonomia.gloss: Stewardship, the management of a household on behalf of its master, and by extension the administration of a plan. The word can describe both the office entrusted to a steward and the arrangement he administers. Paul received a stewardship of God's grace: not a possession to be proud of but a trust to be dispensed for the sake of others, faithfully distributing the riches of Christ to the Gentiles.

mysterion.gloss: A mystery, in the biblical sense not a puzzle or a secret guarded by the initiated, but a truth once hidden in God and now openly revealed. It is a plan too great for human discovery, made known only because God has disclosed it. Here the mystery of Christ is the uniting of Jew and Gentile in one body, a purpose kept in God for ages and now proclaimed to all through the gospel.

synkleronomos.gloss: Fellow heir, one who shares an inheritance jointly with others. Paul stacks three such syn compounds in verse 6, fellow heirs, fellow members of the body, and fellow partakers, to hammer home that the Gentiles do not stand on the margins as second-class dependents but share fully and equally with Israel in every blessing that belongs to those in Christ.

polupoikilos.gloss: Manifold, many-sided, richly varied, literally much-variegated, like an intricately woven or many-coloured fabric. The wisdom of God displayed in uniting Jew and Gentile in one body is not a single flat idea but a masterpiece of many colours, so dazzling that it is put on show even before the rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms, who learn God's wisdom by watching the church.

prothesis.gloss: Purpose, plan, that which is set forth beforehand. The uniting of the nations in Christ is no divine afterthought or emergency measure but the eternal prothesis, the settled purpose God formed before the ages and has now accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord. The whole sweep of history moves within this fixed and gracious design.

parresia.gloss: Boldness, freedom of speech, the confident freedom to approach and to speak openly, without fear or shame. In Christ, and through faith in Him, believers may enter God's very presence with such boldness. The access opened in 2:18 is here felt as glad confidence: the reconciled sinner comes to God not cringing but freely, as a welcomed child of the household.

basic.context: Paul, a prisoner for the sake of the Gentiles, explains the plan behind everything he has said. God entrusted him with a stewardship of grace and revealed to him a mystery hidden for ages: that through the gospel the Gentiles now share fully with Israel as fellow heirs, fellow members, and fellow partakers in Christ. Paul, calling himself the least of all the saints, was given the task of preaching the unsearchable riches of Christ, so that through the church God's many-sided wisdom would be displayed even to the powers in the heavenly realms. All of this is God's eternal purpose, in whom we come to God with boldness.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage is a long digression that flows from the temple picture of chapter two and prepares for the prayer of 3:14. Paul breaks off in verse 1, I, Paul, the prisoner, and resumes the sentence in verse 14. In between he unfolds the mystery and his role in it. The key is to read mystery as Paul defines it in verse 6: not secret knowledge, but the open plan to unite Jew and Gentile in one body through the gospel.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul was given an oikonomia, a stewardship, of God's grace to dispense to the Gentiles. The mysterion, mystery, is the once-hidden, now-revealed plan of God. Its content is that the Gentiles are synkleronomos, fellow heirs, with Israel. Through the church the polupoikilos, manifold, many-coloured wisdom of God is displayed, according to His eternal prothesis, purpose, accomplished in Christ, in whom we approach God with parresia, boldness.
basic.typology: The stewards and prophets of the old covenant who guarded God's word find their fulfilment in the apostles and prophets to whom the full mystery is now revealed. The inheritance once bound up with the land and people of Israel opens out to all nations in Christ, the true heir, so that Gentiles become fellow heirs of the promise made to Abraham.
basic.christ: Christ is the content of the mystery, the mystery of Christ, and the one in whom Jew and Gentile are made fellow heirs. His unsearchable riches are what Paul preaches. He is the one in whom God's eternal purpose is accomplished, and through faith in Him we gain bold access to God. The whole plan of the ages centres and is realised in His person and work.
basic.god: God is the author of a plan formed before the ages and hidden in Himself, the Creator of all things who was working toward this all along. He is a God of manifold wisdom, whose glory is displayed even to the heavenly powers through the church. And He is the God who freely gives grace, to the Gentiles and to the least of the saints, and who now welcomes His people with boldness into His presence.
basic.crossReferences: Colossians 1 speaks of the mystery hidden for ages, now revealed, Christ in you the hope of glory. Romans 16 tells of the mystery kept secret for long ages, now made known to all nations. Galatians 3 makes the Gentiles heirs according to the promise. 1 Corinthians 2 speaks of God's secret wisdom revealed by the Spirit.
basic.oneStory: From the promise to Abraham that all nations would be blessed in his seed, God carried a plan that no one fully saw, guarded through Israel until the seed came. Now the veil is drawn back: in Christ the nations are fellow heirs, and the church becomes the display of God's wisdom before heaven and earth. The story of the ages is the unfolding of one eternal purpose, accomplished in Christ our Lord.

context.literary: This is a great parenthesis. Paul begins to pray in verse 1, for this reason I, Paul, but interrupts himself with a dash of thought that runs to verse 13, then resumes the prayer in verse 14 with for this reason. Within the digression he moves from his own stewardship, verses 1 to 4, to the content of the mystery, verses 5 to 7, to the church's role in displaying God's wisdom, verses 8 to 13. The ellipsis in verse 1 marks the break.
context.historical: Paul writes as a prisoner, likely in Rome, chained for the very message that the Gentiles are included on equal terms with Israel, a claim that had provoked fierce opposition. The largely Gentile Ephesian church needed to see that his sufferings were not a defeat but bound up with the glory of the mystery entrusted to him. The idea that a hidden divine plan was now revealed spoke into a culture fascinated by secret religious knowledge, but Paul insists this mystery is proclaimed openly to all.
context.canonical: The passage belongs with the other mystery texts of the New Testament, Romans 16:25-27, Colossians 1:26-27, and 1 Corinthians 2:6-10, which together present the gospel as God's long-hidden, now-revealed purpose. Its vision of the church displaying God's wisdom to the heavenly powers is unique and expands the cosmic scope of the letter begun in chapters one and two.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the Gentile believers the vast plan behind their inclusion, so they grasp that they are full and equal heirs in Christ, to explain Paul's suffering as the appointed cost of this ministry, and to lift their eyes to the cosmic display of God's wisdom through the church so they are not discouraged.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is largely revelation and explanation: what the mystery is, how it was disclosed, and what God is doing through the church. The abiding truths are the equal standing of all believers in Christ, the centrality of the gospel Paul preached, and the confidence of bold access to God. The call in verse 13 not to lose heart is a genuine exhortation grounded in that revelation.

debates.question: What does Paul mean by the word mystery?

views.label: A once-hidden plan now openly revealed
views.case: Paul defines the mystery in verse 6 as the truth that the Gentiles are fellow heirs through the gospel. It was kept hidden in God for ages, verse 9, and has now been revealed by the Spirit to the apostles and prophets and proclaimed to all. It is not secret knowledge for a select few but God's public purpose made known.

views.label: A body of secret teaching for the initiated
views.case: Some, echoing the mystery religions of the ancient world, might hear mystery as hidden lore reserved for insiders; but Paul's own words rule this out, for the mystery is illuminated for everyone, verse 9, and preached openly to the Gentiles.

debates.bearing: Reading mystery as the revealed plan keeps the gospel public and universal, not esoteric; the wonder is not that the truth is withheld but that it has now been unveiled to all in Christ.

debates.question: Who are the apostles and prophets to whom the mystery was revealed?

views.label: New Testament apostles and prophets
views.case: In context, verse 5, the mystery is revealed now, in contrast to other generations, which points to the New Testament apostles and prophets as the foundational witnesses of the new era, matching 2:20 where the church is built on their foundation.

views.label: The Old Testament prophets read afresh
views.case: Others include the Old Testament prophets, whose writings foresaw the nations' inclusion; yet Paul's stress on now revealed, as it had not been in former generations, favours the new-covenant foundational witnesses.

debates.bearing: Either way the mystery is grounded in God's authorised witnesses, not private speculation; the emphasis of verse 5 falls on the fresh and fuller revelation given in Christ's own generation.

study.originalLanguages: Paul frames his ministry as an oikonomia, a stewardship of God's grace, a household trust to be dispensed for others. Its content is the mysterion, the mystery, not secret lore but the plan hidden in God and now unveiled. The heart of it is that the Gentiles are synkleronomos, fellow heirs, one of three syn compounds in verse 6 stressing full equality with Israel. Through the church God puts on display His polupoikilos wisdom, many-coloured and intricate, before the heavenly powers, all according to His eternal prothesis, purpose, accomplished in Christ. And the fruit for believers is parresia, bold and confident freedom to enter God's presence.

typology.fulfillment: The Old Testament stewards and prophets who guarded God's word are fulfilled in the apostles who now dispense the fully revealed mystery of Christ to the nations.
The inheritance once tied to the land and people of Israel opens to all nations in Christ, so that Gentiles become fellow heirs of the promise to Abraham.
The wisdom celebrated in the Old Testament is displayed in its fullest, many-coloured splendour in the uniting of Jew and Gentile in the church, before the watching heavenly powers.

study.christ: Christ is at the centre of the whole passage. The mystery is the mystery of Christ, and its content is that Jew and Gentile are fellow heirs in Christ Jesus. What Paul preaches to the nations is the unsearchable riches of Christ, treasures too deep to be exhausted. God's eternal purpose is one He accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord, and it is in Him and through faith in Him that believers gain bold access to God. Every strand of God's ancient plan is gathered up and realised in Him.
study.god: God is revealed as the eternal planner, who formed His purpose before the ages, hid it in Himself as the Creator of all things, and has now brought it to light in Christ. His wisdom is manifold, many-sided, and glorious, so rich that He displays it before the rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms through the church. He is lavish with grace, giving it to Gentile outsiders and to the least of the saints alike, and He is the God who now throws open His presence, welcoming His people with boldness.

crossReferences.note: The mystery hidden for ages and generations, now revealed, Christ in you the hope of glory, the sister letter's parallel.
The mystery kept secret for long ages, now disclosed and made known to all nations to bring about the obedience of faith.
In Christ the Gentiles are Abraham's offspring and heirs according to the promise, matching fellow heirs in verse 6.
Things into which even angels long to look, akin to the heavenly powers learning God's wisdom through the church.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story is the unfolding of one eternal purpose. God promised Abraham that all nations would be blessed through his seed, then guarded that plan through Israel while its full shape stayed hidden in Him. In Christ the veil is lifted: the nations are fellow heirs, and the church becomes the stage on which God's many-coloured wisdom is displayed to heaven itself. From a promise whispered to one man to a purpose proclaimed before the powers of the cosmos, the whole story moves toward its accomplishment in Christ Jesus our Lord.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by the mystery in this passage?

options.text: A plan once hidden in God and now openly revealed: that Gentiles are fellow heirs with Israel in Christ.
options.note: Paul defines it in verse 6; it is disclosed and preached to all, not secret lore.

options.text: A truth that remains secret and known only to a select few.
options.note: Paul says it is illuminated for everyone and preached openly to the Gentiles.

options.text: A puzzle that human wisdom eventually worked out on its own.
options.note: It was made known by revelation and the Spirit, not by human discovery.

options.text: A teaching that has nothing to do with the Gentiles.
options.note: The mystery is precisely that the Gentiles are fellow heirs through the gospel.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the Gentiles' standing in verse 6?

options.text: Fellow heirs, fellow members of the body, and fellow partakers of the promise in Christ.
options.note: Three syn compounds stress full and equal sharing with Israel.

options.text: Guests who may observe Israel's blessings from a distance.
options.note: They share fully as fellow heirs, not as onlookers.

options.text: Members on probation until they adopt the law.
options.note: They are already fellow heirs through the gospel, not on probation.

options.text: Permanently excluded from the promise.
options.note: The whole point is their inclusion as fellow partakers of the promise.

quiz.q: What role does the church play according to verse 10?

options.text: Through the church God's manifold wisdom is displayed to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms.
options.note: The church is the theatre of God's many-coloured wisdom before the heavenly powers.

options.text: The church exists mainly to keep social order on earth.
options.note: Its role here is far larger: to display God's wisdom even to heavenly powers.

options.text: The church hides God's wisdom from the world.
options.note: The wisdom is made known, put on display, not hidden.

options.text: The church has no part in God's cosmic purpose.
options.note: Paul places the church at the centre of God's eternal purpose.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe himself in relation to this ministry?

options.text: Less than the least of all the saints, given grace to preach the unsearchable riches of Christ.
options.note: Paul marvels that such grace and stewardship were given to one so unworthy.

options.text: The greatest of the apostles, deserving of the task.
options.note: He calls himself the least of the saints; the stewardship is all of grace.

options.text: A reluctant messenger who resents the calling.
options.note: He counts it grace and calls his sufferings the Gentiles' glory.

options.text: An independent teacher with his own private message.
options.note: He received the mystery by revelation and shares it with the apostles and prophets.

quiz.q: What is the eternal purpose Paul speaks of in verse 11?

options.text: God's settled plan, formed before the ages and accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord.
options.note: The uniting of the nations in Christ is no afterthought but God's eternal purpose.

options.text: A backup plan God devised after His first plan failed.
options.note: It is an eternal purpose, not an emergency measure.

options.text: A purpose still waiting to begin at some future date.
options.note: Paul says it is already accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord.

options.text: A purpose that bypasses Christ altogether.
options.note: The purpose is accomplished precisely in Christ Jesus our Lord.

quiz.q: What confidence do believers have in Christ according to verse 12?

options.text: They may enter God's presence with boldness and confidence through faith in Him.
options.note: The access of 2:18 is felt here as glad, bold freedom to approach God.

options.text: They may approach God only through appointed human mediators.
options.note: Access is through Christ and faith in Him, not through other mediators.

options.text: They must approach God cringing and uncertain of welcome.
options.note: Paul says boldness and confidence, the freedom of a welcomed child.

options.text: They have no real access to God at all.
options.note: In Him we may enter God's presence with boldness and confidence.

questions: Paul calls the gospel a mystery once hidden and now revealed. How does it change your reading of Scripture to see the whole Old Testament quietly carrying a plan now unveiled in Christ?
The Gentiles are fellow heirs, fellow members, and fellow partakers with Israel. Where are you tempted to see any believer as second-class, and how does this equal standing in Christ correct that?
God displays His wisdom to the heavenly powers through the church. How does it lift your view of your own local congregation to know it is part of that cosmic display?

Paul calls himself the least of the saints yet entrusted with unsearchable riches. How does the idea of stewardship, a trust to dispense rather than a possession to keep, reshape how you use the grace you have received?
In Christ you may come to God with boldness and confidence. What would change in your prayers this week if you truly approached God as a welcomed child rather than an anxious outsider?

translationNotes.note: The verse ends with an ellipsis in the BSB because Paul breaks off his sentence, the prisoner of Christ Jesus for the sake of you Gentiles, and resumes the thought in 3:14; the ellipsis marks the interruption and is not a dash.

The Greek stacks three compounds beginning with syn, together: fellow heirs, fellow members of the body, and fellow partakers, underscoring that the Gentiles share fully and equally with Israel in Christ.
Some manuscripts read the fellowship of the mystery rather than the stewardship of this mystery; the sense in either case is the administration and disclosure of God's once-hidden plan.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 3:1-13 reproduced as supplied, including the ellipsis at the end of verse 1. No em or en dashes used.
